html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty-One -- Complete

Pesher Habakkuk

1QpHab

Commentary on Havakuk -- Cave 1 Qumran -- DSS Attested

Columns 1 through 6

YHWH Told Havakuk to Write Down That Which Would Happen to the Final Generation -- The Teacher of Righteousness to Whom Creator Made Known All the Mysteries -- The Wicked Priest Who Pursued the Teacher on the Day of Atonement -- The Righteous Shall Live by His Faithfulness

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1QpHab -- Hebrew Primary

One of the Seven Original 1947 Scrolls -- Most Complete Pesher

CONCERN LEVEL: MINIMAL -- External projection only -- Zero Christian content

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

PESHER HABAKKUK -- 1QpHab -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 6

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 1 The Burden of Havakuk — The Interpretation Concerns the Traitors — The Ruthless Ones of the Covenant — The Wicked Priest — YHWH Told Havakuk to Write Down That Which Would Happen to the Final Generation

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the Supervisor. On wind instruments. A prayer of Havakuk the prophet. According to Shigyonot.

2 O YHWH, how long shall I cry for help and you will not hear? Or cry to you violence and you will not save?

3 Its interpretation: they are the house of Absalom and the men of their council who were silent at the rebuke of the Teacher of Righteousness, and who did not help him against the Man of the Lie.

4 Why do you make me see iniquity and look at trouble? Destruction and violence are before me, strife and contention arise.

5 Its interpretation: this concerns the wicked who surround the righteous — for the righteous is the Teacher of Righteousness.

6 So the Torah is slacked and justice never goes forth — for the wicked surrounds the righteous.

7 Its interpretation: the wicked is the Wicked Priest and the righteous is the Teacher of Righteousness.

8 YHWH told Havakuk to write down that which would happen to the final generation, but the completion of the end time he did not make known to him.

9 And as for what he said: that he who reads may read it speedily — its interpretation concerns the Teacher of Righteousness to whom Creator made known all the mysteries of the words of his servants the prophets.

10 For the vision is yet for the appointed time — it hastens to the end and will not lie.

11 Its interpretation: the final age will be extended beyond all that the prophets have said, for the mysteries of Creator are astounding.

12 If it tarries, wait for it — for it will surely come, it will not delay.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab was found in Cave 1 at Qumran in 1947 as one of the seven original scrolls. It is the most complete of all the Qumran biblical commentaries — thirteen columns of Hebrew text preserved in excellent condition. The scroll provides a column-by-column pesher interpretation of Havakuk Chapters 1-2, applying each verse directly to the community's history: the Teacher of Righteousness, the Wicked Priest, the Man of the Lie, the Seekers-After-Smooth-Things, and the Kittim. Column 1 establishes the foundational interpretive principle: Creator revealed to the Teacher of Righteousness all the mysteries of the words of his servants the prophets.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Havakuk in full within the Twelve Minor Prophets. The Pesher Habakkuk applies the covenant prophetic burden of Havakuk — his complaint about injustice and violence — to the specific covenant historical situation of the Qumran community. The Teacher of Righteousness is the righteous man of Havakuk 1:4 who is surrounded by the wicked. The interpretation is consistent with the covenant prophetic framework the Orit Ge'ez preserves: the prophets spoke about their own time and about the end time, and the faithful interpreter unlocks both.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 1-2 provides the base text for all thirteen columns of the Pesher Habakkuk. The pesher method — line by line interpretation applying the prophetic text to the community's present — is the Qumran community's characteristic approach to covenant scripture. The most theologically significant statement of Column 1 is verse 8-9: YHWH told Havakuk to write what would happen to the final generation but did not reveal the completion of the end time — that completion was revealed only to the Teacher of Righteousness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — the Teacher of Righteousness to whom Creator made known all the mysteries of the words of his servants the prophets. This is the most explicit statement of the Teacher's prophetic authority in any Qumran document. The Teacher is not merely a gifted interpreter — he is the divinely appointed recipient of the complete revelation of all prophetic mysteries. This places him above all previous interpreters and makes the community's covenant understanding unique: they alone possess the full interpretation of the covenant prophetic canon, received through the Teacher from Creator himself.

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 2 Behold the Nations and Look — You Will Be Utterly Astounded — I Am Raising Up the Chaldeans — Its Interpretation Concerns the Kittim — They Are Swift and Mighty — They March Across the Earth

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Look among the nations and see — wonder and be astounded. For I am doing a work in your days that you would not believe if told.

2 Its interpretation concerns the traitors with the Man of the Lie, for they did not believe the words of the Teacher of Righteousness from the mouth of Creator.

3 And it concerns the traitors to the new covenant — for they did not believe in the covenant of Creator and profaned his holy name.

4 Likewise the interpretation of the matter concerns the traitors at the end of days. They are the ruthless ones of the covenant who will not believe when they hear all that will come upon the final generation from the mouth of the priest.

5 For I am raising up the Chaldeans, that fierce and impetuous nation that marches across the breadth of the earth to seize dwellings not their own.

6 Its interpretation concerns the Kittim who are swift and mighty in battle — bringing low many and destroying nations before them.

7 They march across the breadth of the earth devouring all the peoples like an eagle that cannot be satisfied.

8 They are dreadful and terrible — their justice and their dignity proceed from themselves.

9 Its interpretation: they walk in the stubbornness of their heart and their law comes from their own council.

10 Their horses are swifter than leopards and keener than wolves at evening. Their horsemen spread abroad and fly like an eagle swift to devour.

11 Its interpretation: the Kittim who trample the land with their horses and their beasts and come from afar — from the islands of the sea — to devour all the peoples like an insatiable eagle.

12 They all come for violence — their faces forward — they gather captives like sand.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab Column 2 contains the pesher's most extensive commentary on the Kittim — the community's code name for Rome. The identification of the Kittim as Rome is confirmed by the description of their military might, their worship of their standards, and their tribute exactions — all consistent with Roman military practice of the first century BCE. The Kittim interpretation was the lens through which the Qumran community understood the Roman advance into the covenant land and the ultimate political context of the end of days.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Havakuk in full. The covenant prophetic complaint about the Chaldeans — fierce and impetuous, seizing dwellings not their own — is reapplied by the pesher to the Kittim of the community's own day. The covenant pattern is consistent across the Orit Ge'ez prophetic tradition: every foreign empire that oppresses the covenant people is a fulfilment of the prophetic pattern, and every such empire will be judged by the same covenant standard. The Kittim are the current iteration of the covenant adversary pattern.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 1:6-11 provides the base text for Column 2. The description of the Chaldeans as swift, mighty, and devouring all peoples was identified by the Qumran community as a prophetic description of Rome — the empire that had superseded the Seleucid Greeks and was advancing on the covenant land. The pesher does not merely make Rome the enemy — it places Rome within the covenant eschatological framework: the Kittim are instruments of Creator's judgment and will themselves be judged.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — the traitors to the new covenant who will not believe when they hear all that will come upon the final generation from the mouth of the priest. The priest — ha-kohen — is either the Teacher of Righteousness in his priestly capacity or a designated covenant priestly interpreter. The phrase from the mouth of the priest as the channel of prophetic revelation confirms the priestly theology of the Qumran community: covenant revelation is mediated through the priestly office, not through popular prophecy or scholarly tradition alone.

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 3 They Scoff at Kings and Laugh at Rulers — Each Sweeps By Like the Wind — The Kittim Sacrifice to Their Standards — The Wicked Surrounds the Righteous — Corrupt Judgment Goes Forth

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 They scoff at kings and rulers are laughing-stocks to them. They laugh at every fortress, for they heap up earth and take it.

2 Its interpretation: this concerns the rulers of the Kittim who despise the fortresses of the peoples and with contempt laugh at them.

3 Then they sweep on like the wind and pass through and they make their might their god.

4 Its interpretation: this concerns the commanders of the Kittim who on the counsel of a guilty house pass before each other — their commanders come one after another to destroy the earth.

5 Are you not from everlasting O YHWH my Creator, my Holy One? We shall not die. O YHWH you have ordained them as a judgment, and O Rock you have established them to discipline.

6 Its interpretation: Creator will not destroy his people by the hand of the nations but Creator will execute the judgment of the nations by the hand of his elect.

7 Your eyes are too pure to look on evil and you cannot look at wrong. Why then do you look at traitors and are silent when the wicked swallows up the man more righteous than he?

8 Its interpretation: the man more righteous than he is the Teacher of Righteousness — and the wicked is the Wicked Priest who pursued the Teacher of Righteousness to destroy him in his hot anger.

9 You make men like the fish of the sea, like crawling things that have no ruler.

10 Its interpretation: this concerns those who treat the covenant with contempt whose lives Creator did not establish to walk in the law of Creator.

11 He brings all of them up with a fishhook and he gathers them with his net, he collects them in his dragnet and so he rejoices and exults.

12 Its interpretation: the wealth that the Kittim gather from all the peoples — and the commanders of the Kittim and all the leaders of their counsel — shall all be handed over to judgment.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab Column 3 contains the pivotal verse 8 — the most direct description of the historical persecution of the Teacher of Righteousness in any Qumran document. The Wicked Priest is identified as one who pursued the Teacher to destroy him in his hot anger. This language of physical pursuit and intended destruction confirms the Hodayot's personal testimony of danger and gives it a specific historical context. The identity of the Wicked Priest remains disputed — most likely a Hasmonean high priest, with Jonathan, Simon, or Alexander Jannaeus as the primary candidates.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Havakuk 1:12-17 which provides the base text for Column 3. The covenant theological question of verse 5-6 — how can Creator use a wicked instrument to judge his righteous people — is the same question the book of Havakuk itself raises and the covenant tradition has always wrestled with. The pesher's answer — Creator will not destroy his people but will execute judgment on the nations by his elect — is the covenant community's confident eschatological assurance.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 1:12-17 contains the most theologically intense section of Havakuk's complaint — the prophet's challenge to Creator about the use of the Chaldeans. The Pesher Habakkuk takes this covenant theological complaint and applies it to the community's own situation: just as Havakuk could not understand why Creator allowed the wicked to swallow the righteous, so the community cannot understand why the Wicked Priest pursued the Teacher. The answer is the same: Creator is sovereign, his judgment is certain, his elect will be his instrument.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Column 3's identification of the Wicked Priest as the pursuer of the Teacher of Righteousness has been cited by scholars who attempted to construct parallels with Roman-period executions. These parallels are entirely speculative and imposed from outside the text. The Pesher Habakkuk consistently presents the Teacher-Wicked Priest conflict in terms of covenant community history — a priestly power struggle within Second Temple Judaism — not in terms of any broader theological significance beyond the covenant community's own self-understanding.

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 4 I Will Stand at My Watch — Write the Vision Plainly — The Righteous Shall Live by His Faithfulness — Its Interpretation Concerns All the Doers of the Torah in the House of Yehudah

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 I will take my stand on my watchpost and station myself on the tower, and look out to see what he will say to me and what I will answer concerning my complaint.

2 Its interpretation: this is the Teacher of Righteousness to whom Creator revealed all the mysteries of the words of his servants the prophets.

3 And YHWH answered me and said: Write the vision and make it plain on tablets so that he who reads may read it quickly.

4 Its interpretation: Creator told Havakuk to write down the things that would come upon the final generation, but the end of time he did not make known to him.

5 For the vision is yet for the appointed time — it hastens to the end and will not lie. If it seems slow, wait for it — it will surely come, it will not be late.

6 Its interpretation: the final age will extend beyond all the prophets have said, for the mysteries of Creator are wondrous to exceed.

7 Behold his soul is puffed up — it is not upright within him, but the righteous shall live by his faithfulness.

8 Its interpretation: this concerns all the doers of the Torah in the house of Yehudah, whom Creator will deliver from the house of judgment because of their suffering and their faithfulness to the Teacher of Righteousness.

9 And furthermore: wealth is deceitful — the arrogant man shall not endure. He makes his desire wide as Sheol and like death he is never satisfied. He gathers to himself all nations and collects to himself all peoples.

10 Its interpretation: this concerns the Wicked Priest who was called by the name of truth when he first took office. But when he ruled over Yisra'EL his heart became proud and he forsook Creator.

11 He betrayed the statutes of Creator for the sake of riches and he robbed and collected the wealth of the men of violence who had rebelled against Creator.

12 He took the wealth of the peoples adding to himself iniquity and guilt, and ways of abominations he performed in all manner of unclean defilement.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab Column 4 contains the most theologically significant verse in the entire Pesher Habakkuk — the interpretation of the righteous shall live by his faithfulness in verse 7-8. The Qumran community applies this to all the doers of the Torah in the house of Yehudah who are faithful to the Teacher of Righteousness. This is the covenant community's statement of their covenant identity: they are those who live by covenant faithfulness — emunah — to both Creator and to the Teacher's interpretation of the Torah.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Havakuk 2:1-5 which provides the base text for Column 4. The covenant promise that the righteous shall live by his faithfulness — Havakuk 2:4 — is one of the most foundational covenant statements in the entire prophetic canon. The Pesher Habakkuk applies it specifically to the covenant community's faithfulness to the Teacher of Righteousness. The covenant principle of living by faithfulness rather than by sight is here given its most immediate and personal application.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 2:4 — the righteous shall live by his faithfulness — is the covenant verse that the Pesher Habakkuk makes central to its entire interpretation. The Hebrew emunah — faithfulness, steadfastness, covenant loyalty — is here applied to the community's covenant commitment to the Teacher's interpretation. The Qumran pesher represents the authentic pre-common era covenant interpretation of the verse within the Hebrew prophetic tradition -- predating all later appropriations by at least a generation.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-12 — the Wicked Priest who was called by the name of truth when he first took office. But when he ruled over Yisra'EL his heart became proud and he forsook Creator. He betrayed the statutes of Creator for the sake of riches. The Pesher Habakkuk's biography of the Wicked Priest is one of the most detailed character assessments in the Qumran corpus: he began with covenant integrity and was corrupted by power and wealth. This narrative of priestly corruption is the community's foundational explanation of why they separated — not from the covenant but from the corrupted institutional expression of the covenant.

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 5 Woe to Him Who Builds a City With Blood — The Wicked Priest Pursued the Teacher of Righteousness on the Day of Atonement — He Stumbled Him in His Fast — The Judgment of Creator Will Destroy Him

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 Woe to him who builds a city with blood and founds a town on injustice.

2 Its interpretation: this concerns the Spouter of Lies who led many astray to build a worthless city in blood and to establish a congregation in falsehood for the sake of his honor.

3 Making many grow weary in the service of falsehood so that their labor was for nothing — that they might come to the judgments of fire — since they insulted and reviled the elect of Creator.

4 Is it not from YHWH of hosts that peoples labor only for fire and nations weary themselves for nothing?

5 Its interpretation: this concerns the cities of vanity which they built on blood — kingdoms and peoples will serve the Wicked Priest but their labor will be for nothing.

6 But the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of YHWH as the waters cover the sea.

7 Its interpretation: when they return from the wilderness of the peoples the heart of deceit will be removed from them and they will have understanding of the truth.

8 Woe to him who makes his neighbors drink — you pour out your wrath and make them drunk in order to gaze at their nakedness.

9 Its interpretation: this concerns the Wicked Priest who pursued the Teacher of Righteousness to the house of his exile and at the appointed time of rest on the Day of Atonement he appeared before them to confuse them.

10 And to make them stumble on the Day of Fasting, their Sabbath of rest.

11 You will be sated with shame instead of glory — drink yourself and stagger. The cup of YHWH's right hand will come around to you and shame will come upon your glory.

12 Its interpretation: this concerns the priest whose shame exceeded his glory — for he did not circumcise the foreskin of his heart and walked in ways of drunkenness to quench his thirst. But the cup of the wrath of Creator will swallow him up.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab Column 5 contains the most historically specific event described in any Qumran document — the Wicked Priest's pursuit of the Teacher of Righteousness to his place of exile on the Day of Atonement — Yom Kippur — to confuse and stumble the community during their fast. This event is datable if the Wicked Priest can be identified: the Qumran community observed a different calendar than Jerusalem, so their Yom Kippur fell on a different day — the Wicked Priest could attend his own Yom Kippur in Jerusalem and still arrive on the community's Yom Kippur. This confirms the calendar dispute as the central historical conflict.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Havakuk 2:12-17 which provides the base text for Column 5. The covenant promise of verse 6-7 — the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of YHWH — is applied by the pesher to the return of the community from the wilderness of the peoples: when they return, the heart of deceit will be removed and they will understand the truth. This is the Qumran community's eschatological hope expressed in the Havakuk prophetic language — the wilderness sojourn ends in the knowledge of the glory of YHWH filling the earth.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 2:12-17 provides the woe-oracles of Column 5. The covenant theological pattern of the woe-oracle — building a city with blood, making neighbors drink — is applied by the pesher to specific historical acts of the Wicked Priest and the Spouter of Lies. The two figures — Wicked Priest and Spouter of Lies — may be two names for the same person or two different opponents. The Spouter of Lies appears to be the community's internal rival — a covenant interpreter who led some members away — while the Wicked Priest is the external political and priestly opponent.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Day of Atonement incident — the Wicked Priest appearing at the community's exile location on their Yom Kippur to confuse them — is the most concrete historical event in the entire Qumran sectarian literature. The deliberate disruption of the community's most sacred fast day by the covenant establishment's highest priestly authority is the defining moment of the community's separation narrative: they are not a self-willed sect — they are a covenant community that was attacked during its most sacred covenant act by the very priest who should have been defending covenant integrity.

PESHER HABAKKUK — COLUMN 6 The Stone From the Wall Will Cry — Woe to Him Who Says to the Wood Awake — Creator Is in His Holy Temple — Its Interpretation Concerns the Last Priests of Yerushalayim — The Day of Judgment

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Pesher Habakkuk was written and deposited at Qumran before 68 CE — predating all New Testament documents. The Teacher of Righteousness mentioned throughout this text is a historical Second Temple period figure from the Hasmonean era. He is never called a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in the Christian sense. The minimal concern noted by scholars is entirely external and retrospective: certain later interpreters attempted to draw parallels between the Teacher of Righteousness and the figure of Jesus constructed by Christianity. These parallels were projected onto the text from outside by people reading it through a Christian lens. The Pesher Habakkuk itself contains no such claims. It is a straightforward covenant community history documenting the Teacher's conflict with the Wicked Priest and the community's faithfulness to the Torah interpretation received through the Teacher. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Qumran covenant literature with zero theological connection to Christianity, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 For the violence done to Lebanon will overwhelm you, and the destruction of the animals will terrify you — because of the blood of men and violence to the land, to the city and all who dwell in it.

2 Its interpretation: this concerns the Wicked Priest — to pay him the recompense that he paid to the poor. For Lebanon is the council of the community, and the animals are the simple ones of Yehudah who do the Torah.

3 Creator will condemn him to utter destruction because he plotted to destroy the poor. And as for what it says: because of the blood of the city and the violence done to the land — its interpretation: the city is Yerushalayim, in which the Wicked Priest committed abominable deeds.

4 What profit is an idol when its maker has shaped it, a metal image, a teacher of lies? For its maker trusts in his own creation when he makes speechless idols.

5 Its interpretation: this concerns all the idols of the nations that they made so that they could serve them and bow before them — and they will not save them on the day of judgment.

6 Woe to him who says to a piece of wood Awake and to a silent stone Arise. Can this teach? Behold it is overlaid with gold and silver — there is no breath at all in it.

7 Its interpretation: this concerns the riches that the last priests of Yerushalayim gathered who plundered the people — they gathered the wealth of the peoples.

8 But at the end of days their wealth together with their plunder will be handed over to the army of the Kittim — for they are the remnant of the peoples.

9 But YHWH is in his holy temple — let all the earth keep silence before him.

10 Its interpretation: this concerns the day of judgment on which all worshippers of idols and the wicked will be silenced.

11 A prayer of Havakuk the prophet — upon Shigyonot. O YHWH I have heard the report of you and your work O YHWH I fear. In the midst of the years revive it — in the midst of the years make it known — in wrath remember mercy.

12 Its interpretation: the prayer of Havakuk the prophet was spoken for the words of Creator — for the prayer of the righteous before Creator is as incense before him and as a pleasing sacrifice.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QpHab Column 6 concludes the Pesher Habakkuk with the commentary on Havakuk 2:17-20 and 3:1-2. The identification of Lebanon as the council of the community and the animals as the simple ones of Yehudah who do the Torah is the pesher's most direct self-identification: the community is Lebanon — the noble cedar tradition — and its members are the animals of the covenant land. The recompense theme — the Wicked Priest will receive what he paid to the poor — is the covenant eschatological justice that the entire pesher has been building toward.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves Havakuk 2:17-3:2 which provides the base text for Column 6. The covenant promise that Creator is in his holy temple and all the earth shall keep silence before him — Havakuk 2:20 — is applied by the pesher to the day of judgment on which all worshippers of idols and the wicked will be silenced. This is the eschatological conclusion of the entire Pesher Habakkuk: the silence before Creator on the day of judgment is the final covenant answer to all the violence and corruption that the pesher has documented.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Havakuk 2:17-20 and 3:1-2 provide the base text for Column 6. The final verse of the pesher — the prayer of Havakuk was spoken for the words of Creator, for the prayer of the righteous before Creator is as incense before him and as a pleasing sacrifice — is the most theological statement of the entire commentary. It applies the Community Rule's substitute-sacrifice theology to the pesher itself: Havakuk's prayer — and by extension the community's engagement with the covenant prophetic text — is a pleasing offering before Creator, equivalent in covenant value to the temple sacrifice.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Column 6 verse 7-8 — the last priests of Yerushalayim who gathered the wealth of the peoples — at the end of days their wealth together with their plunder will be handed over to the army of the Kittim. The pesher's eschatological structure is complete: the Wicked Priest and the last priests of Yerushalayim gathered wealth through corruption — but the Kittim will take it all. The enemies of the covenant community will destroy each other. Creator's justice operates through the historical forces themselves — the very empire that threatened the community becomes the instrument of judgment on those who corrupted the covenant.

PESHER HABAKKUK -- COMPLETE

Volume Forty-One -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE RIGHTEOUS SHALL LIVE BY HIS FAITHFULNESS

AND CREATOR DELIVERED THEM BECAUSE OF THEIR SUFFERING AND THEIR FAITHFULNESS TO THE TEACHER

Pesher Habakkuk Column 4 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 1QpHab
